1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Strategy"
     3→part: 3
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1614-1630"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**STRATEGY**
     9→
    10→**Role of Strategy in a corporate**  
    11→[Strategy plays a crucial role in management by defining how an organization will perform and achieve long term success.](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=4902e191eed173f17e4d21d1a3f7115ab23bcf42ec927b02a4cdcfcdef8b8582JmltdHM9MTc2OTkwNDAwMA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=4&fclid=215cdef7-1735-6765-2800-c80416176699&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly9jb3Jwb3JhdGVmaW5hbmNlaW5zdGl0dXRlLmNvbS9yZXNvdXJjZXMvbWFuYWdlbWVudC9jb3Jwb3JhdGUtc3RyYXRlZ3kv&ntb=1)             
    12→
    13→[Strategy is a comprehensive plan to achieve goals in the face of complex                       conditions, including social, political, economic, and competitive environments.It defines how an organization will achieve its goals and involves a significant               commitment of resources.](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=4902e191eed173f17e4d21d1a3f7115ab23bcf42ec927b02a4cdcfcdef8b8582JmltdHM9MTc2OTkwNDAwMA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=4&fclid=215cdef7-1735-6765-2800-c80416176699&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly9jb3Jwb3JhdGVmaW5hbmNlaW5zdGl0dXRlLmNvbS9yZXNvdXJjZXMvbWFuYWdlbWVudC9jb3Jwb3JhdGUtc3RyYXRlZ3kv&ntb=1)  
    14→   
    15→Strategic Leadership is a very important portfolio for positioning a company in a direction of competitive superiority thereby facilitating sustainability, growth and prosperity of the company. Hence positioning the organization strategically is very important to the business objectives of the organisation.
    16→
    17→It is said that with good strategy, half the game is already won. For example, why does a cricket team or a football team need a coach even if the team comprises of very good players, very skilled for their respective positions. It is because the coach coordinates the teamwork amongst his players and formulates techniques utilizing the best of different skills of the players to beat the opponent and win. This formulation is called the strategy. Good players are of course required, but not enough to win a match. If good players are made to follow a strategy, the match is won. 
    18→
    19→However, a point to be noted is that a strategy evolved should be such that the opponent will not be able to know it before, otherwise the opponent will be careful to thwart such strategies. Therefore strategies need to be changed from time to time so that the opponent becomes unaware of your moves from time to time. Sometimes, you may have seen in a T20 cricket match, a spinner opens the bowling for a team. This is to create confusion to the opening batsman who is usually good at facing fast pace bowlers. The bowling team would expect that the opening batsman would either falter or lose his wicket or he may play so carefully that no runs can be scored, runs being so vital during the initial overs. This may be a strategy of a team. However if this strategy is repeated over time, the opponent can learn and thwart such strategy. Therefore strategies need to change from time to time.
    20→
    21→In business too, it is difficult to win amidst stiff competitors. Strong competitors may have the capital, manpower, equipment, production capability and marketing abilities. But strategy provides the winning edge over others. Therefore, formulating strategy is of significant importance. Without strategy, it is difficult to win and survive even if you have the best of all things in your organization.
    22→

1→---
     2→title: "THE ONE HARVARD MAN"
     3→part: 3
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1631-1652"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE ONE HARVARD MAN**
     9→
    10→I have often found that an organisation recruits a top rated highly educated person as the CSO (Chief Strategy Officer), sometimes from reputed institutions like the Harvard University. With all due respect to Harvardians, however good degree a person holds from any reputed university, the CSO must be acquainted with the laws of the country, the business of the company, the industry and the competition so that the CSO can formulate his strategy accordingly to beat its competitors.
    11→
    12→Say, a CSO gathers idea of a particular business of an organization, takes into consideration of the intricacies of the competition and considers various inputs from other Heads and the Middle Management and eventually comes up with a strategy which may have a winning edge over others. However, is it possible for him to do so proficiently in all the other businesses of the company and formulate a winning strategy for them?
    13→
    14→In big conglomerates like L\&T, Tata group companies, etc. there are many different Business Units catering to widely varied different businesses. The business environment, competitors and the industry for each of the Business Units is entirely unique and different. In such a case, what does the CSO do? He consults the Heads and seniors of these businesses and replicates the strategy as narrated by them because it is not possible to deep dive and learn the nitty-gritties of all businesses. Being supposedly qualified for the post, he may have nothing in original in his thought process and his strategy becomes just a copy of what the Heads of other Business Units echo and he presents these with complex graphs and coloured titles through PowerPoint presentations to the Board.  
    15→   
    16→Therefore, what I am trying to convey is that a single CSO of a company cannot do justice for all the business units of the organization. As I mentioned earlier, good strategy leads to winning, not good players alone. And since procuring business is the first prime objective of a company after which execution and operations come into play thereby generating revenues and profit, one CSO cannot be entrusted to formulate strategies of all Business Units. There must be different CSOs for different businesses or one CSO for a group of allied Business Units. Only then these CSOs can formulate effective strategies for different Business Units with the help of respective Heads. 
    17→
    18→You may have a single CFO or a single CPO in an organization since the principles of accounting or financing (CFO) or the principles of Procurement (CPO) remains the same. But so far Strategy is considered, the principles widely vary with different businesses based on different competitive environment. Therefore a single CSO cannot do justice to all kinds of businesses of the company. 
    19→
    20→There is a tendency in some multi-disciplinary organisations to keep a single CSO for all Business Units. But as explained above, such CSOs cannot do proper justice to all varied businesses. This is what I call the “One Harvard man” phenomenon. The word “Harvard” is used because it is renowned, however, in spite of that one such qualified person cannot do justice to all business units, hence the label “one Harvard man”. As illustrated above, you must have a group of CSOs engaged with different Business Units so that you can concentrate wholly on one or some allied business only and formulate effective strategies for them. This is very important because I keep on reiterating that good Strategy leads to winning, sustenance and growth and therefore strategy formulated for all different business units must be at the highest level. Strategy must be considered as a top priority of the organization. It is like the heart of a human being; once it stops beating the body is lifeless however good your other organs are. Without excellent strategy, there can be no business, and without business an organization cannot exist, as simple as that. 
    21→
    22→I again refer to a simile with a cricket or a football team. The eleven players of a team cannot be equally best players; some will be bound to be better than others and all will have different talent. It is the coach along with the captain who will form the strategy to use his players to win. Similarly, this coach in the organization is the CSO. His role and importance is above other CXOs simply because if he is able to bring business to the company then only other CXOs have a role to play. And just like there are different coaches in a cricket team, there have to different CSOs appointed for different business so that there is no compromise in formulating the best strategies for each specific business. 
    23→
    24→**The salient points are:**
    25→
    26→1. A single CSO cannot formulate effective strategies for all varied businesses of an organization.   
    27→2. Keep a number of CSOs attached to different kinds of businesses. Do not rely on a single CSO to deliver the best strategies for all businesses  
    28→3. Revenues and profits are realized through operations; Operations come to play only when you acquire business; Acquiring business depends on your strategies you formulate; Hence the immense importance of CSO. Other CXOs have a role in operations only after the CSO and its team acquires business for the organization
    29→

1→---
     2→title: "THE POINTPOWER PRESENTATION"
     3→part: 3
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1653-1672"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE POINTPOWER PRESENTATION**
     9→
    10→Once a senior person associated with CSO asked me to fill up a Strategy Paper presentation with different questions on them. However, even as an Executive Vice President and Business Head, I could not understand the head or tail of it. Whatever it was, I was sure it was not related to the context of Indian environment and business. It was a strategy paper presentation with questions copied from a reputed foreign university probably relevant to other kind of business in a foreign country.
    11→
    12→But then, why such a paper was given to me? Later on I understood that the sender wanted to “point” the “Power” of his paper indicating the enormous theoretical knowledge he and his Strategy Department possess. “Pointing the Power”, hence I call it a “PointPower” presentation instead of the well understood “PowerPoint” presentation.
    13→
    14→Often people in high positions tend to show off their knowledge by copying strategic presentations from a foreign institution of repute of which they actually don’t have knowledge about. They also fail to understand whether such presentations have any significance in the Indian business environment. Such indulgence is ridiculous and a sheer waste of time.  
    15→   
    16→So, let me finish the story. What did I do after receiving the “PointPower” presentation? I replied, “It must be a great thought, I have never seen such a paper before. However I am incompetent to fill up the same. What I can do is to provide you any business data that you wish to receive from me. But then, you please fill up the paper”. You know what happened after that. Knowing it is difficult for him also to fill up and will make no sense even if it’s filled up, I was told after a few days, “Leave it, please put up your strategy paper in your own way as you deem fit in line with your business and competition”
    17→
    18→I learnt the sarcastic way to stab such indulgence. Never show off with “PointPower” presentations where you yourself do not know the head or tail of it. Give simple, easy to understand PowerPoint presentations which make sense for people to understand the strategies one must adopt to win business.  Make simple PowerPoint presentation comprising of great innovative ideas with verified data to understand strategies, don’t throw “PointPower” presentations to show your wisdom which you actually you do not have.
    19→
    20→Also Heads must understand that a CSO is not the best experienced person in your field of expertise. With the help of the Heads for understanding the business and competition, the CSO can use his expertise to formulate the strategies. So never criticize a CSO for not having the business knowledge as a Business Head may have. The CSO need not have that in the first place. A CSO is like the coach of a cricket or a football team. The coach of the team may not have been a very good player like the players of the team, but essentially he may have the best ability to formulate the strategy for the team in consultation with his team players.
    21→
    22→**The salient points are:**
    23→
    24→1. Provide simple “Powerpoint” presentations with verified data to formulate strategy of your business  
    25→2. Strategy presentations should be simple and easy to understand comprising of great innovative ideas which are implementable  
    26→3. Providing powerful strategy presentations copied from elsewhere and irrelevant to your country and business environment to show your wisdom is sheer waste of time. Such “Pointpower” presentations do not work at all. All employees can eventually evaluate your true wisdom in some time to come, so waste no time in pretending to have the same by providing complicated presentations to your colleagues.
    27→

1→---
     2→title: "Some Good Practices"
     3→part: 3
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1673-1689"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Have a crystal clear vision**  
    11→A strategist must have a clear vision as to what techniques need to be employed to achieve goals amidst stiff competition. The current market situation and environment must be before his eyes for developing the strategies.
    12→
    13→**Avoid overconfidence**  
    14→Do not get swayed away by great performance of the past. Your competitors will be strategizing to counter your past success in the future, so you have to be careful. The past strategy may not work in future. Therefore you may need to devise new strategies with time.
    15→
    16→**Train strategists**  
    17→Good strategists are not necessarily the best performers. In fact they are not meant to be so. They have a vision of future and idea of competition based on which they evolve strategies to win. That is their domain expertise. They can study the strategies of different competitors and align their organization not only to counter the strategies of the competitors but also evolve new strategies from time to time which the competitors will be unaware of. 
    18→
    19→**Understand different business domain**  
    20→Strategists must take help of business leaders to understand the domain and competition of their business and evolve strategies to counter them and remain on the top. An organization must have a number of strategists to develop the best strategy in different domains of business. They can communicate the same to their top leadership and the BoD for insights to way forward.
    21→
    22→**Develop both end strategies**  
    23→Strategise to build more to your strength as well as reduce more on your weakness. Developing strategies at both ends will significantly boost your overall strength to compete in the market
    24→

1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Marketing & Business Development"
     3→part: 4
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1692-1704"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**MARKETING & BUSINESS DEVELOPMENT**
     9→
    10→**Role of Marketing & Business Development in a corporate**  
    11→[The Marketing department in a corporate plays a critical role in promoting the company's products or services and attracting new customers. Key                responsibilities include market research, advertising, public relations, branding,product development, sales support, and customer relationship management. ](https://www.bing.com/ck/a?!&&p=b0ad471c9a0eb3eb2fd8a0fd7194c65f0621554044cfcb739a191c667233fe08JmltdHM9MTc2OTkwNDAwMA&ptn=3&ver=2&hsh=4&fclid=215cdef7-1735-6765-2800-c80416176699&u=a1aHR0cHM6Ly9lbWVyaXR1cy5vcmcvaW4vbGVhcm4vd2hhdC1pcy10aGUtaW1wb3J0YW5jZS1vZi1tYXJrZXRpbmctZm9yLWJ1c2luZXNzLw&ntb=1) 
    12→
    13→Marketing & Business Development department is the foundation for growth and sustenance of any organisation. Everyone’s career including that of the Managing Director and the Chairman depends on their success. If they bring orders, then only the existence of employees has a meaning. Without orders, what will the organisation execute? Without orders, the organisation has to shut down after the current projects or orders are completed.
    14→
    15→Therefore a continuous order book in an organisation is necessary for its survival, growth and sustenance. However, too many of them at the same time will be catastrophic to the organisation. If you have too many orders at the same time, you may not have adequate people, finance and infrastructure to execute. Suddenly recruiting people, purchasing equipment and taking financial loans is not a solution because such enhanced assets may not be required later on when order levels are low. Similarly, too less of orders are another problem for the organisation since the entire employees, finance and the infrastructure of the organisation remains under utilised.
    16→
    17→Therefore it’s really a difficult job for this Department. They cannot take too many or too less orders but the question is they cannot get orders whenever they want and space them as wished. Orders are not off the shelf products that whenever you wish you buy them. You have to participate in tenders in a disciplined and a regulated manner so that orders keep trickling in a phased manner. With just adequate orders, an organization can utilize manpower, equipment, finances and other infrastructure optimally. This should be the target of any organization.
    18→
    19→Marketing could be direct or through bidding. For commodities, you have to resort to marketing your products through direct contacts and advertisements. For projects announced by the government and private companies, you need to participate in bidding or an auction and become L1. The bidder who fulfills the techno-commercial requirements of the client for its product and service is called a techno-commercial responsive bidder. In such techno-commercial responsive bidders, the bidder quoting the lowest price to the client is said to be the L1 bidder. Business Development (BD) is a process through Marketing. Once you can market your products and services through direct selling or through participation in tendering, you develop your business.
    20→

1→---
     2→title: "THE ENGLISH MAN IN MARKETING"
     3→part: 4
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1705-1738"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE ENGLISH MAN IN MARKETING**  
     9→   
    10→Many senior professionals in the industry believe that one who can speak in English and know some clients by virtue of his past interactions in different projects must be a good Marketing professional. As if knowing English and the client are the main criteria for being a good marketing personnel\! I sometimes don’t understand why such senior people live in a fool’s paradise. Sometimes they speak wrong English which they themselves are not aware of. For example, I heard a senior person elevated to a Head saying in a meeting, “the company has bidded …….”. The Head did not know that the past tense of “bid” is “bid” itself. Maybe he thought that adding a “ed” to the noun ‘bid’ will make it a verb “bidded” just like the words “head to headed”, “guide to guided”, “manipulate to manipulated”, “construct to constructed” and hence “bid to bidded”. I was so ashamed on realizing as to what the client would think about that person and the company itself.
    11→
    12→Elevating an employee with knowledge of “so called” English and a particular client to head Marketing without acquiring the basic skills of the discipline spells disaster to an organization. The disaster would lead to a lack of orders or acquiring bad orders and spoil the business. Let me explain you why.
    13→
    14→Bidding, Marketing and Business Development (BMBD Unit) are interrelated subjects. All these three streams focus on getting good and quality orders for the organisation, hence it is the most important Unit of an organisation driven by the Strategy Business Unit. Who can book orders in a stiff competitive market? Only those, who have real time experience in the past in such BMBD Units.
    15→
    16→The success of a BMBD Unit is not dependent on knowing English or a friendly client. It depends on possessing knowledge and having experience in diversified areas like:
    17→
    18→(1) Knowledge of the market based on his years of experience  
    19→(2) Knowledge of the pattern of competition over the years  
    20→(3) Knowledge of the pulse and levels of aggressiveness of the competitors  
    21→(4) Market intelligence through different sources  
    22→(5) Knowledge of bidding tricks to reduce price without compromising quality   
    23→(6) Knowledge on how competitors are optimising costs  
    24→(7) Knowhow of new methods, innovation and modern techniques  
    25→(8) Knowledge of cheaper funding sources  
    26→(9) Knowledge of prospective suppliers and contractors as partners  
    27→(10) Knowledge to develop win-win partnerships  
    28→(11) Acumen and vision to strategise a winning bid
    29→
    30→Above acquisition of knowledge and experience takes time to learn, understand and practice. Hence one has to be necessarily experienced in the BMBD Unit for past several years to acquire such knowledge and experience or become the Head of BMBD Department. In fact, his knowledge has to be the best in the industry to beat competitors and become a techno-commercial responsive L1 bidder. Hence English and knowing friendly clients will not at all help. English is only a medium to communicate.
    31→
    32→**EXPERIENCE COUNTS\!**
    33→
    34→**The salient points are:**
    35→
    36→1. Bidding, Marketing and Business Development (BMBD) is a specialized Department to acquire orders for an organization  
    37→2. Like all expertise, BMBD professionals too require specialized expertise and experience  
    38→3. BMBD is the most important Department in an organization guided by the Strategy Department because once it brings in orders, only then all employees of different Departments can be engaged in their works  
    39→4. Anyone knowing English and a client cannot become a BMBD professional. English is only a medium to communicate
    40→

1→---
     2→title: "THE DANGEROUS SPY"
     3→part: 4
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1739-1760"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE DANGEROUS SPY**
     9→
    10→The Marketing Department fetches huge amount of data from different departments. For any particular bid, it has to fetch manpower data, equipment data, vendor cost data, subcontractor cost data, labour cost data, design data, finance cost data, etc. It also has the company’s overhead cost data and intended profit margin so that the bid price can be developed with all the data in hand. 
    11→
    12→In the pursuit to quote low with reasonable margins and become a responsive L1 bidder, you need to optimise all the above data to reach at a most competitive bid price. It is now clearly understood that such data for the organization needs to be kept absolutely secret. In other words, these data should not be known to any competitor, otherwise they may too use the data to win their bids.  
    13→   
    14→Sometimes, there could be an unethical employee in the Department, with his fellow colleagues not knowing that he is unethical, who divulge these secret data to its competitors in exchange of money. So dangerous they are, on one side they earn money from the organisation as an employee, and on the other side they earn money by revealing secrets to its competitor. Since such an unethical guy earns money from competitors, he has no concern that his organization will not be able to bag the order. He has no interest for his organization but have interest to fill his own pocket.
    15→
    16→The Marketing Head have to keep his eyes and ears open. They also must have some sort of internal intelligence to know the mischief of such employees. And once known, HR should simply sack them in line with the company policy.
    17→
    18→So far the price factor is concerned; I know how a Leader used to keep such data secret till the submission of the bid. Usually the technical and the price bid are prepared at a junior level. The junior level fills up the various price schedules for supply, transportation and service, and finally fills up the Summary Price Schedule based on unit rates given to him by the seniors (quantities may already be specified by the client if it is not a turnkey project). This Summary indicates the final bid price for the project which the junior level is now aware of.
    19→
    20→So what does the Leader do? He provides escalated unit rates to the junior manager to fill up the Price Schedules and asks to seal the envelope. Then the Leader himself or a senior manager estimates the actual price that would have been based on actual unit rates. Thereafter the leader prepares a Discount Letter indicating the discount in amount or as a percentage to be applied on the Final Price indicated on the sealed envelope based on the estimate with actual unit rates. The Leader himself seals the Discount Letter in another envelope and asks his employee to submit both the envelopes together to the client. In this way, the Junior manager is not able to know the final discounted price of the bid.
    21→
    22→There are other unethical means that marketing personnel can adopt. A vendor may influence a Head that if the Head proposes the particular vendor and not anyone else in the bid, then the Head would earn a certain amount of money in case the bid is successful and the organisation gets the order. Such acts may compromise the quality and price of the bid. The Head also takes a risk because in case the vendor is not competitive, then his bid too will not be competitive. It’s unethical for an employee to resort to such activities putting his own organisation in danger. The Head should be considering the most competitive vendors to win a bid and not entertain one particular vendor in lieu of money. 
    23→
    24→**The salient points are:**
    25→
    26→1. An undertaking must be taken by BMBD professionals to keep all data secret just like it happens in military organisations  
    27→2. Secrecy of bidding data is a key to success in winning bids against its competitors  
    28→3. Unethical divulgence of bidding data to competitors or elsewhere is a  serious offence which calls for serious action against the employee
    29→

1→---
     2→title: "THE MANIPULATED SPECIFICATION"
     3→part: 4
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1761-1786"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE MANIPULATED SPECIFICATION**
     9→
    10→You have learnt by now that the Marketing team needs to develop a competitive techno-commercial responsive bid. But sometimes a Head or any individual may try to influence a client for incorporating such a clause which will debar some other bidders to qualify and participate in the bidding process. Therefore the competition will be limited to only a few bidders. A clause found irreverent in line with the project requirement and which debars some competitors to qualify, will lead to questions on the integrity of the client. 
    11→
    12→Let me give an example. Suppose in a particular industry, there are quite a few competitors in existence for last 10 years who have done extremely well in the projects they have executed and also executing projects satisfactorily currently. Along with these competitors there could be another two or three old timers with more than 20 years of experience who are equally well experienced and competitive. Now in order to reduce the competition, one of the old timers might try to influence the client that in order to get the best project execution, the client should invite bidders who have at least 15 years of experience. Now, the requirement of 15 years, if considered in the specification, will debar the good contractors who have only 10 years of experience. Such a clause has no bearing on the quality of project execution because since a contractor with 10 years of experience too can execute the project very well. This is what I call the manipulation of specifications.
    13→
    14→Another example can be as follows. A big trader supplies a particular product by associating with a competitive manufacturer through exclusive agreement. This means that the manufacturer can sell his product only through this exclusive trader. Now in a bid inviting supply of such products, another manufacturer might adopt unethical means to influence a client for incorporating a clause that the tender should invite only manufacturer bidders and traders will not be allowed to participate. In such a case, the competitive trader is out of competition because he is not a manufacturer. 
    15→
    16→The client’s focus should be to get the least priced techno-commercially responsive product. It should not be interested whether the product shall be delivered by a trader or a manufacturer. Hence incorporating such a clause is insignificant and is meaningless. 
    17→
    18→In another example, a bid required to supply a few quantity of a kind of tested structure whose designs and data had been given by the client in the specifications. According to the designs, any bidder could manufacture the structure in their plant. But a prospective bidder found out a way to restrict another very competitive bidder to bid. That bidder influenced the client to incorporate a clause which stated that the bidder must have test facilities to test such structures. Since the other competitive bidder did not have the test facilities, he would be debarred from participating in the tender. Now since the supply project did not require testing, is the clause for having test facilities relevant? 
    19→
    20→There might be innumerous examples of such malpractice adopted by big competitors to reduce competition. You can easily understand whether certain clause debarring some competitors to participate is relevant or not.
    21→
    22→What can you do in such a case? You should approach the client in writing stating that such clauses are not relevant to the experience and execution of the project. In case it is a big important project for you, you can file a petition in the court of justice.
    23→
    24→Such practices of incorporating irreverent clauses cannot happen without the connivance of the contractor and the client.
    25→
    26→**The salient points are:**
    27→
    28→1. Convincing client by manipulating through for incorporation of irreverent clauses in bidding documents is an offence.  
    29→2. Bidders being debarred from participation in bidding due to such irreverent clauses may give a written representation to the client  
    30→3. Bidders having no respite even after representations may register a complaint to the Chairman or to the government if the client is a government organisation.
    31→

     2→title: "THE CARTELISATION"
     3→part: 4
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1787-1807"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE CARTELISATION**
     9→
    10→Cartelisation is teaming up together amongst competitors to stop competing against each other. It’s highly unethical.
    11→
    12→It happens in two ways. If competitors think that they should not compete against each other for similar products, then they may team up to keep almost similar prices so that the public can choose anyone of them. In such a cartelisation, all the competitors have an advantage? Let me explain. Say,   
    13→a company keeps his price extremely low than others to allure most customers but in this case the competitor has to do business with meager margins or no margins. The revenues may be high but the margins will be very meager. Remember, margins are important because the cost is incurred, only margins help an origination to sustain and grow and also importantly earn salaries for its employees. The other competitors may not have customers due to higher price but is the catalyst to drive the lower priced company to do business with little or no margins.  
    14→   
    15→So when this company teams up with others and price their product or service at similar price levels but at higher price levels than what the lowest priced company had priced, all the companies earn revenues and decent margins. In this way all the companies get some business and the business is distributed amongst all companies and all of them make reasonable margins to sustain and grow their organisations.
    16→
    17→In the market of groceries, you shall find many similar products priced exactly or almost the same. Is it by chance? How could it be that even the overhead costs of all the companies are same? It is difficult to believe that cost of manufacturing and overhead costs are exactly the same for so many companies. I cannot prove, but a question arises in our minds, is it a cartel?
    18→
    19→In bids for projects, there is a slight difference in the cartelisation method. Suppose similar kind of projects is coming up for bidding by various clients across the country. The bidders (ie. the competitors) may team up and assign between themselves one project each for each bidder, meaning in the one project assigned to one particular bidder, the other bidders intentionally quote high making the assigned bidder win. Similarly the same phenomenon is repeated for subsequent projects with projects assigned to each bidder turn by turn.
    20→
    21→Such cartelisation is highly unethical. The client should analyse winning patterns and be able to notice such cartels and take strong action against the bidders. The client, on suspicion of a cartel existing, can also call for a rebid. In such a phenomenon, since the competitors are actually not competing against each other, the client does not receive the least price. Hence the client loses financially. If the client is a government organisation, the government actually loses the exchequer.
    22→
    23→**The salient points are:**
    24→
    25→1. Cartelisation is a malpractice though all the companies gain  
    26→2. The client lose by not receiving the least price   
    27→3. Client can call for a rebid if suspicious of a cartel existing
    28→

     3→part: 4
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "1808-1827"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE PRE-BID SURVEY BLUNDERS**
     9→
    10→When a tender is announced by an authority, the bidding team starts preparation of the bid documents. The Manager needs to quote its best for the supplies and services involved in the project. Say for a high voltage transmission line from place A to place B, you need to send a survey team who would traverse the line physically by car and across foot, to observe the soil conditions, availability of labour in that region, an appropriate place to establish the site stores, the climate and working conditions prevailing across the line, the obstacles like rivers and highways that the line has to cross, etc. This is called the pre-bid survey.
    11→
    12→Now, the Designs of foundations, which form a significant cost of the transmission line project, are based on the bidding parameters as well as the soil conditions seen during the pre-bid survey. The designs will go for a toss in case the soil conditions observed are widely wrong than what it actually is. Why this may happen? It can be if the engineer has misread the soil condition, or did not take care to undertake a soil test if the conditions observed by him are critical, or he may have assumed the same after seeing some patches of soil conditions along the line. It can also happen if the engineer is negligent and traverses by car for long distances ignoring intermediate places through which the transmission line would pass.
    13→
    14→To avoid all these lapses, the engineer may be asked to carry a pocket GPS so that the Project Manager can track the survey route of the engineer. Also, photographs and videos may be captured so that the Project Manager can discuss with the engineers regarding soil conditions and if soil tests are felt necessary.
    15→
    16→The point is pre-bid survey forms a basis of costing particularly for the services portion of the contract. If the pre-bid survey goes haywire then the costing too goes haywire and the organisation may face immense problems regarding the cost estimated for the project. The pre-bid necessarily needs to be conducted in a very minute manner and the engineer must discuss all issues with the Project Manager in order to arrive at a correct estimate of the cost of the project.
    17→
    18→Sometimes, it so happens that the Project Management Head does not want to send any Project Engineer for the survey due to lack of manpower and relies only on the bidding engineer for the survey details. This is dangerous because a Project Engineer has far more expertise in the survey of fields, land, soil conditions and routes than the bidding engineer and such carelessness of the Project Management Head must be avoided. It is this Project Management Head who may complain against the Bidding team after award in case blunders are discovered later on which were committed by the Bidding team at the time of pre-bid survey ignoring the fact that it was he himself who had refused to send a Project Engineer along with the bidding engineer for the pre-bid survey. Such blame games serves no purpose since the company now has to face the risk of the cost estimate.    
    19→
    20→**The salient points are:**
    21→
    22→1. Pre-bid survey of a project site must be relied on the findings of a Project Engineer, not a bidding engineer since a Project engineer possess better expertise of site supervision  
    23→2. Tests like soil tests should be done if there is doubt after eye inspections  
    24→3. Pre-bid survey forms the basis of cost estimate, particularly for the services portion of a project, hence its importance  
    25→4. Project Management Head should be in continuous touch with the Project engineer entrusted with the pre-bid survey so that all data required for cost estimate is gathered and not missed
    26→

     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Know your target audience**  
    11→You must be clear who you would like to be your customers with clear reasons. Targeting all possible customers will fail your marketing strategy. Marketing must be customer or a group of customer specific.  
    12→
    13→**Consider data and analytics**  
    14→Current marketing data and analytics will provide insights to your marketing strategies. These data are dynamic and therefore need to be referred from time to time.
    15→
    16→**Interact with Bidding Manager**  
    17→Sometimes a Marketing Manager may take decisions which are difficult for the Bidding Manager to consider and incorporate in his bid. Therefore the Marketing Manager must ensure that his decisions shall be in compliance with the bidding procedures. For example, a Marketing manager intends to tie up with some Joint Venture partners in order to qualify the PQ criteria laid down in the bidding specifications. But he may not be aware that a JV partner may not be allowed in a bid to qualify for a bid as per the bidding documents. 
    18→
    19→**Review developments of forthcoming business**  
    20→Progress in possible forthcoming business must be reviewed. Time and money spent for due diligence of forthcoming business must commensurate with the likely business estimated to be procured.
    21→
    22→**Allot different manpower for different regions**  
    23→Particularly for international marketing, someone may have more knowledge and expertise in the European market and someone may have more expertise in the African market. The market dynamics and business environment may differ widely in different regions of the globe. Hence different Marketing may be entrusted for different regions so that each can concentrate on each market dynamics
    24→
    25→**Physical marketing**  
    26→In today’s world, digital marketing is essential. But physical marketing cannot be ignored. It is easier to build trust and authenticity through physical marketing while digital marketing will provide a better outreach.

    10→A top leader sometimes may lack innovative ideas but to prove his acumen he goes ahead with advertisements which lack appeal. When an advertisement strikes your mind or conscience, it is said to have appealed to you and only then you tend to buy the product or service.
    11→
    12→A top leader may also try to save costs of advertisement, which by and large, is a good habit in corporate business. However, the cost curtailment should not be to an extent which will render the advertisement ineffective. So here comes the issue of cost-benefit analysis.
    13→
    14→Mindless cost curtailment in advertisements may lead to no benefits. In order to obtain tangible benefits, a certain minimum amount of money has to be spent in order for the advertisement to be effective. So, the cost-benefit analysis tries to find out what are the additional benefits with respect to sale of the product or service being advertised that you would get versus the money spent on the advertisements. When the cumulative of profit element of the initial sales amount equals the cost of advertisement, you reach the breakeven point. Beyond that, the profit element in the additional sales would ensure that you have now started realizing profits.
    15→
    16→It is obviously understood that advertisements are meant to achieve additional sales. And if the profits realised after the breakeven point is far more than had there been no advertisements, then only advertisements get success.

    18→I should not name the companies whose advertisements have appeal and whose does not but I will cite one which majority of people acknowledge. So we have little time to view through the content of an advertisement. That is why they should be short and witty.

    24→What a Head can do is to consult an Advertising expert to prepare a witty and a short advertisement for his product or service based on his basic inputs so that it can successfully address the targeted customers.
